frames.caption: Seed acc = 0 (the XOR identity). All three bit columns start at 0; we will XOR each value in.
frames.note: acc starts at 0 because 0 is the XOR identity: a^0 = a. So the first value drops straight into the accumulator unchanged, and from there each new value either lights up the columns it owns or cancels columns a twin already lit.

frames.caption: v = 4 (bits 100): about to compute acc ^= 4. Highlighted columns are the bits 4 will toggle.
frames.note: XOR (^) is 1 in a bit column exactly when an odd number of inputs had that bit set. Fold it across the whole array and every value that appears an EVEN number of times contributes 0 to each column (a^a=0), so duplicates erase themselves no matter where they sit in the stream — XOR is commutative and associative, so order does not matter.

frames.caption: acc ^= 4 -> acc = 4 (bits 100). 4's bits are now folded into acc.
frames.note: XOR (^) is 1 in a bit column exactly when an odd number of inputs had that bit set. Fold it across the whole array and every value that appears an EVEN number of times contributes 0 to each column (a^a=0), so duplicates erase themselves no matter where they sit in the stream — XOR is commutative and associative, so order does not matter.

frames.caption: v = 1 (bits 001): about to compute acc ^= 1. Highlighted columns are the bits 1 will toggle.
frames.note: XOR (^) is 1 in a bit column exactly when an odd number of inputs had that bit set. Fold it across the whole array and every value that appears an EVEN number of times contributes 0 to each column (a^a=0), so duplicates erase themselves no matter where they sit in the stream — XOR is commutative and associative, so order does not matter.

frames.caption: acc ^= 1 -> acc = 5 (bits 101). 1's bits are now folded into acc.
frames.note: XOR (^) is 1 in a bit column exactly when an odd number of inputs had that bit set. Fold it across the whole array and every value that appears an EVEN number of times contributes 0 to each column (a^a=0), so duplicates erase themselves no matter where they sit in the stream — XOR is commutative and associative, so order does not matter.

frames.caption: v = 2 (bits 010): about to compute acc ^= 2. Highlighted columns are the bits 2 will toggle.
frames.note: XOR (^) is 1 in a bit column exactly when an odd number of inputs had that bit set. Fold it across the whole array and every value that appears an EVEN number of times contributes 0 to each column (a^a=0), so duplicates erase themselves no matter where they sit in the stream — XOR is commutative and associative, so order does not matter.

frames.caption: acc ^= 2 -> acc = 7 (bits 111). 2's bits are now folded into acc.
frames.note: XOR (^) is 1 in a bit column exactly when an odd number of inputs had that bit set. Fold it across the whole array and every value that appears an EVEN number of times contributes 0 to each column (a^a=0), so duplicates erase themselves no matter where they sit in the stream — XOR is commutative and associative, so order does not matter.

frames.caption: v = 1 (bits 001): about to compute acc ^= 1. Highlighted columns are the bits 1 will toggle.
frames.note: Watch a pair cancel: when the second copy of a value arrives, every bit it sets is already set in acc from its twin, so a^a turns those columns back to 0. The accumulator literally rewinds the contribution of the first copy — that is why only the unpaired value can survive.

frames.caption: acc ^= 1 -> acc = 6 (bits 110). The second 1 cancelled its twin: those columns flipped back toward 0.
frames.note: Watch a pair cancel: when the second copy of a value arrives, every bit it sets is already set in acc from its twin, so a^a turns those columns back to 0. The accumulator literally rewinds the contribution of the first copy — that is why only the unpaired value can survive.

frames.caption: v = 2 (bits 010): about to compute acc ^= 2. Highlighted columns are the bits 2 will toggle.
frames.note: Watch a pair cancel: when the second copy of a value arrives, every bit it sets is already set in acc from its twin, so a^a turns those columns back to 0. The accumulator literally rewinds the contribution of the first copy — that is why only the unpaired value can survive.

frames.caption: acc ^= 2 -> acc = 4 (bits 100). The second 2 cancelled its twin: those columns flipped back toward 0.
frames.note: Watch a pair cancel: when the second copy of a value arrives, every bit it sets is already set in acc from its twin, so a^a turns those columns back to 0. The accumulator literally rewinds the contribution of the first copy — that is why only the unpaired value can survive.

frames.caption: Loop done: the two 1s and two 2s cancelled, so acc = 4 — the single number. Return it.
frames.note: After folding all five values the two 1s and two 2s have each cancelled to 0, leaving only the bits of 4. acc = 4 is the unique number — found in one O(n) pass with O(1) extra space, beating the O(n)-space hash-set approach.
